🟡 Forever Sabah

Case Study
Y
links
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGZcco_p_s/SGmpZwnbMJKz-03C8c8GgQ/edit

Activities
33314415-10e1-81d3-9d0c-cdfb70578f13, 33314415-10e1-816b-8501-e729f39a7fc3, 33314415-10e1-8196-bacd-d8c05d555d8d, 33314415-10e1-81a2-9210-e794d43de95f, 33314415-10e1-81fc-92a8-e444763baae6, 33314415-10e1-81ee-91cb-e99406fb70e5
background_section
## Background Sabah lies on the northern third of Borneo—rainforest, reefs, and Mount Kinabalu—home to many Indigenous communities, especially Dusun peoples, whose land rights and livelihoods face extraction and climate pressure. Industrial palm and timber have cleared large areas: [more than 30% of primary forest since the 1970s](https://www.canva.com/design/DAGZcco_p_s/SGmpZwnbMJKz-03C8c8GgQ/edit). Fragmentation has disrupted wildlife movement, strained watersheds, and weakened cultural and economic foundations for those communities. ### Origins and Network Forever Sabah grew from a two-year, cross-sector question: where Sabah lands in fifty years on a business-as-usual path. That process set priorities and early projects. It operates less as a conventional NGO than as a [social collaborative movement](https://www.canva.com/design/DAGZcco_p_s/SGmpZwnbMJKz-03C8c8GgQ/edit) steering a **25-year** shift toward a diversified, equitable circular economy. ### Philosophy & Approach Change is not treated as something grants alone can buy. The group foregrounds shared experience and “currencies” such as responsibility, participation, reciprocity, and trust to align actors at scale. A **forever** horizon and an Indigenous imperative—to belong to and be accountable to place—run through the programme design.
Sources

patterns

Place
Sabah, Malaysia
place
Sabah, Malaysia
activities
Human-Elephant Harmony|Watershed Conservation and Payment for Ecosystem Services|Renewable Energy Transition|Mangrove Conservation and Spatial Planning|Legal Innovation and Policy Development|Learning and Capacity Building
description
A 25-year transformational program facilitating Sabah's shift to a diversified, equitable circular economy. Operating as a social collaborative movement rather than a traditional NGO, they weave connections across watersheds, forests, and coastal zones.
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy The 25-year programme still aims at a diversified, equitable circular Sabah. Climate responses are increasingly institutionalised, yet communities remain on the front line. Watershed work, mangrove and riparian care, rice revival, renewable energy—threads that once ran in parallel—now need to be joined, scaled, and woven into mitigation and adaptation in a coordinated way.
website
https://www.foreversabah.org/
bioregional_context
Sabah occupies the northern portion of Borneo, the world's third-largest island, characterized by ancient rainforests, diverse marine ecosystems, and Mount Kinabalu. This biodiversity hotspot has faced extensive deforestation due to industrial palm oil and timber extraction, losing over 30% of its primary forest since the 1970s.
category
Bioregional Organizer
